access.accessMethod: Folger bibliographic record and USTC edition record
access.entityLabel: Henry Charteris's 1593 Edinburgh print of The Testament of Cresseid

bibliographic.citation: Robert Henryson, The Testament of Cresseid, compylit be M. Robert Henrisone, Scotaishman (Edinburgh: Henry Charteris, 1593).
bibliographic.title: The Testament of Cresseid

entities.description: The earliest surviving complete Scottish text and, in current METS scholarship, the best witness, while still showing expected late-sixteenth-century editorial and compositorial intervention. The unique known copy is British Library C.21.c.14.

holding.notes: Unique known copy; the Folger record is a vendor bibliographic record rather than a Folger holding.

entities.label: Henry Charteris's 1593 Edinburgh print of The Testament of Cresseid

notes: The print itself has signatures and leaves but no modern printed pagination 2–24.

rights.basis: The work and all known early witnesses predate modern copyright by centuries.
rights.component: Henryson's fifteenth-century underlying literary work
rights.jurisdiction: United States

bibliographic.citation: The Poems of Robert Henryson, ed. G. Gregory Smith, vol. III, Text vol. II, Scottish Text Society (Edinburgh and London: William Blackwood and Sons, 1908).
bibliographic.pages: The Testament of Cresseid, 2–24; Thynne text, 175–198
bibliographic.title: The Poems of Robert Henryson

entities.description: Scottish Text Society edition, volume III overall and text volume II. The Testament occupies printed pages 2–24 and presents Charteris's 616-line text with editorial interventions; a Thynne text and comparison appear at pages 175–198 and in the comparison table.
entities.label: G. Gregory Smith, The Poems of Robert Henryson, volume III (1908)
notes: HenCres PB milestones exactly match Smith pages 2–24. Readings such as XML line 216 ‘blak, callit philologie,’ XML line 178 without Smith's supplied ‘[gay],’ and XML line 6 ‘can’ show that Smith is not the unqualified text source.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1908, and the Internet Archive item provides unrestricted public download.
rights.component: Smith's 1908 edition and the accessed scan
rights.jurisdiction: United States

access.entityLabel: METS scholarly census of witnesses to The Testament of Cresseid

entities.description: The witness discussion in the current Middle English Texts Series introduction, used here as an aggregate scholarly description so that fragmentary, missing, complete, and late derivative witnesses are not forced into one false stemma.
entities.label: METS scholarly census of witnesses to The Testament of Cresseid
notes: The census includes a hypothetical early Scottish print but the evidence reviewed here does not justify creating that print as a confirmed entity.

entities.description: The Asloan manuscript's c. 1515–1525 contents record a copy of the Testament, but the corresponding leaves are now missing. It is an attested lost copy, not an extant textual witness available for collation.

holding.notes: The codex survives; the leaves containing the listed Testament do not.

entities.label: Lost Testament leaves once in the Asloan manuscript (NLS MS 16500)

access.entityLabel: The Testament in William Thynne's 1532 Works of Chaucer

bibliographic.citation: The workes of Geffray Chaucer newly printed, with dyuers workes whiche were neuer in print before, ed. William Thynne (London, 1532), fols. 219r–222v.
bibliographic.title: The workes of Geffray Chaucer

entities.description: The earliest surviving complete text, printed anonymously at folios 219r–222v after Troilus and Criseyde. It is substantially anglicized and the Complaint is textually disordered.
entities.label: The Testament in William Thynne's 1532 Works of Chaucer

entities.description: A single seven-line stanza corresponding to Testament lines 561–567 appears on inserted leaf/page 92b under a heading misattributing it to ‘Bocas.’ The manuscript was assembled in the Fortingall area by the MacGregor circle between 1512 and 1542.

entities.label: Book of the Dean of Lismore, NLS Adv. MS 72.1.37, Testament excerpt

physicalDescription.extent: 159 folios plus fragments; the work survives as one seven-line stanza
notes: The stanza appears on a small inserted leaf identified by ISOS as page 92b.

entities.label: Bodleian Library MS Add. C. 287, Kynaston Testament transcription and Latin translation

entities.description: Four stanzas corresponding to lines 582–609 were added on leaf (9r) by Joseph Holland, apparently from a Speght edition, in a manuscript of Chaucer's works. The work occurrence is an excerpt, not a complete Testament.
entities.label: Cambridge University Library MS Gg.4.27(1), four-stanza Testament excerpt
notes: The four-stanza extent is established by scholarship and direct inspection of CUDL canvas 1084; an aggregate work-level line count in a discovery index must not be misread as this witness's extent.

next_steps: Ask the Oxford Text Archive and U-M for the accession record for U-243-A.
Do not infer that current A03023 is the same digital encoding or that the repository-root Unlicense overrides the legacy source notice.
openQuestions.question: What accession, permission, and transformation record links legacy OTA U-243-A, the U-M CME item, and the repository XML, and how should its personal-scholarly-use notice be reconciled with U-M's current NoC-US statement?

next_steps: Trace the inventory and bibliographical arguments cited by current METS scholarship before creating any lost-print entity or dependency edge.
openQuestions.question: Did a lost early-sixteenth-century Scottish print mediate the surviving Testament tradition before Thynne 1532?

next_steps: Consult the cited early-modern-reader scholarship and manuscript descriptions before adding unmodeled Speght edition entities and direct copied-from edges.
openQuestions.question: Which exact Speght impressions and readings mediate each later manuscript copy, especially CUL Gg.4.27(1), beyond the secure L.1-from-1602 and Add. C. 287-from-1598 statements?

reviewNotes: The 1593 heading in HenCres.xml is a reproduced imprint layer, not the work's composition date or a second title-bearing work.
Smith 1908 is modeled as the source of page milestones and as a collated edition, not as the source of every XML reading.
The lost Asloan leaves, fragmentary witnesses, complete prints, Speght-derived copies, and modern digital reproductions are not conflated.
The current OTA A03023 EEBO-TCP text is a separate 2002–2003 encoding of the same 1593 print, not a renamed or proven continuation of legacy OTA U-243-A.
Public access, underlying-text copyright, modern image rights, license claims, and legacy provider restrictions are recorded separately.

lineage.summary: The repository artifact is a copy or version of University of Michigan/OTA electronic text U-243-A, created in 1993. Its textual base is Henry Charteris's 1593 Edinburgh print, the earliest surviving complete Scottish witness and the best early witness according to current METS scholarship. Its PB milestones reproduce pages 2–24 of G. Gregory Smith's 1908 edition, but direct comparison shows that the XML does not simply adopt all of Smith's emendations. The lineage therefore separates textual witness, pagination source, scholarly edition, electronic encoding, repository artifact, modern facsimiles, and collateral manuscript and print witnesses.

catalogSummary.author: Robert Henryson
catalogSummary.date: Composed in the late fifteenth century; exact date unknown
catalogSummary.editor: No single editor; Charteris 1593 is the source witness and Smith 1908 supplies pagination
catalogSummary.region: Scotland; narrative set during the Trojan War
catalogSummary.title: The Testament of Cresseid

catalogingSubject.label: The Testament of Cresseid
catalogingSubject.scope_note: Robert Henryson's late-fifteenth-century Older Scots poem as an intellectual work. The 1593 Charteris print, later editions and excerpts, manuscript witnesses, modern facsimiles, and the U-M/OTA and EEBO-TCP encodings are transmission layers rather than separate works. ‘The Complaint of Cresseid’ is an internal passage, not a second work.

contentParts.label: Narrative before Cresseid's complaint
contentParts.summary: The narrator invokes Chaucer, recounts Cresseid's abandonment and return to her father, and narrates her dream of the planetary parliament, judgment, disease, exclusion, and removal to the leper hospital.

contentParts.label: The Complaint of Cresseid
contentParts.summary: Cresseid reviews her former status, acknowledges her fall, laments her present exclusion, and warns other lovers.

contentParts.label: Closing narrative, testament, and death
contentParts.summary: After the complaint, Cresseid goes begging, encounters Troilus without mutual recognition, learns the almsgiving knight was Troilus, makes her testament, dies, and receives a tomb whose epitaph records her fall.

dateStatements.display: Late fifteenth century; exact date unknown, before Henryson's death around 1500
notes: The value 1480 in the repository XML is a legacy encoding assertion, not adopted here as a secure exact date.

dateStatements.display: Recorded in the Asloan manuscript contents, c. 1515–1525; the listed Testament leaves are now missing
notes: This is evidence for an early copy or title, not an extant textual witness and not the date of composition.

dateStatements.display: 1532, in William Thynne's edition of Chaucer's works
notes: Thynne preserves the earliest surviving complete text but anglicizes it and prints it anonymously after Troilus and Criseyde.

extent.description: One poem modeled in three narrative units: 58 seven-line stanzas before the Complaint, seven nine-line Complaint stanzas, and 21 seven-line closing stanzas. The repository XML contains 86 LG elements and 617 L elements because an ungrouped final ‘Finis’ is encoded as L N=617.

assertion.notes: The repository XML also encodes a final ungrouped ‘Finis’ as L N=617; it is a terminator, not a 617th verse line.

formStatements.description: A 616-line poem in 86 stanzas: 79 seven-line rhyme-royal stanzas and seven nine-line stanzas in the Complaint.

genres.label: Tragedy
notes: The narrator calls the work a tragedy.

genres.label: Chaucerian continuation
notes: The label places the poem in the reception tradition of Chaucer's Troilus and Criseyde; Henryson supplies an independent alternative ending rather than a textual continuation or version of Chaucer's poem.

genres.label: Complaint
notes: Restricted to lines 407–469, the seven nine-line stanzas spoken by Cresseid; the XML's second DIV1 heading extends beyond the complaint through the closing narrative.

languageStatements.label: Older Scots
notes: Often described more specifically as Middle Scots. Witnesses and editions may be substantially anglicized; that transmission-layer variation is not assigned to the work as a whole.
The stanza and line counts describe the intellectual poem and are reconciled explicitly with the repository encoding's extra L element for ‘Finis’.

Witness dialect, scribal spelling, Charteris's compositorial and editorial interventions, Smith's emendations, and digital encoding practices remain in the lineage manifest rather than being collapsed into work language or authorship.
The six supplied line-range DIV2 headings in HenCres.xml are encoding navigation, not treated as a stable authorial section hierarchy.

next_steps: Consult current Henryson chronology and textual scholarship before adding normalized composition bounds or a precise place.
openQuestions.question: Can the poem's composition be dated or localized more narrowly than late-fifteenth-century Scotland without treating literary inference or Henryson's uncertain biography as documentary fact?

next_steps: Review the inventory and bibliographical evidence cited in the METS introduction; do not create a confirmed witness entity from a hypothetical lost print.
openQuestions.question: Did a now-lost early-sixteenth-century Scottish print transmit the Testament before or independently of Thynne 1532?

notes: This describes the work's literary and linguistic context; it does not claim a securely documented place of composition.
The attribution is stable even though Henryson's exact life dates and much of his biography remain uncertain.

summaries.value: After invoking Chaucer and another account of Cresseid, the narrator tells how she is abandoned by Diomede, denounces the gods, and is judged in her dream by a planetary parliament with leprosy and exclusion. At the leper hospital she laments her fall in a seven-stanza complaint; afterward, while begging, she encounters Troilus without either recognizing the other. Once told his identity, she makes a testament to the lepers and dies; Troilus receives her ring and erects her tomb. Henryson transforms the unresolved fate of Chaucer's Criseyde into a Scots tragedy about mutability, social judgment, repentance, compassion, and literary authority.

This record describes Henryson's poem, not the 1593 Charteris imprint reproduced in the XML heading, Smith's 1908 editorial presentation and pagination, or the U-M/OTA encoding. The XML's supplied division label ‘The complaint of cresseid’ covers lines 407–617, but the complaint proper occupies only lines 407–469; lines 470–616 return to third-person narrative.

notes: The title is used by every complete early witness according to the METS scholarly introduction; its spelling is normalized here only for catalog display.
Discovery title of the U-M/CME electronic item, not an independently attested work title.
titles.value: Robert Henryson's The testament of Cresseid

work.author: Robert Henryson
work.dateDisplay: Composed in the late fifteenth century; exact date unknown
work.editor: No single editor; Charteris 1593 is the source witness and Smith 1908 supplies pagination

publication.embeddedTitle: Robert Henryson's The testament of Cresseid

work.regionDisplay: Scotland; narrative set during the Trojan War
work.summary: After invoking Chaucer and another account of Cresseid, the narrator tells how she is abandoned by Diomede, denounces the gods, and is judged in her dream by a planetary parliament with leprosy and exclusion. At the leper hospital she laments her fall in a seven-stanza complaint; afterward, while begging, she encounters Troilus without either recognizing the other. Once told his identity, she makes a testament to the lepers and dies; Troilus receives her ring and erects her tomb. Henryson transforms the unresolved fate of Chaucer's Criseyde into a Scots tragedy about mutability, social judgment, repentance, compassion, and literary authority.
